text: The video displays the herniation of the Roux limb and omentum into the hiatus. The surgeon begins by using a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions between the omentum and the hiatus, with visible assistance from traction and contra-traction techniques.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to lyse adhesions between the small bowel and the peritoneal sac, employing a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions and divide the posterior aspect of the peritoneal sac. Traction and contra-traction techniques are employed to facilitate the complete division of the sac.
The surgeon removes the peritoneal sac from the Abdallah Kafti after completely excising it, following the division of the sac using a harmonic scalpel.

A flexible endoscope is inserted, providing a clear view of the gastric pouch. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down adhesions between the gastric pouch and the pleura, facilitating the mobilization of the gastric pouch into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon re-approximates the hiatal defect using three figure-of-eight sutures with 2-0 Surgidac, employing an in-the-stitch suturing device. The robust tissue comes together without tension during the closure process.

Following the completion of the posterior cruroplasty, the surgeon places synthetic bio-absorbable mesh to reinforce the repair. The mesh is sutured to the diaphragm using multiple interrupted two-zero Surgidac sutures, with the indoor stitch autosuturing device.
The surgeon reinforces the repair by suturing a piece of omentum to the diaphragm using a two-zero Surgidac and a running stitch.
The intraorbital endoscopy reveals a large ulcer within the gastric pouch, with a visible tablet situated in the crater of the deep ulcer.
